title: Interdependence

content.question: Which isolated problem are you solving that's actually connected to 3 others?
content.business_lens: Marketing affects product; product affects retention.
content.life_lens: Sleep affects weight; stress affects skin.
sop: Pick a problem you've been treating as isolated.
List 2-3 other areas it's connected to, and rate each connection's strength 1-10 (see calculation, page 4).
Trace which of those connected areas might actually be the root cause.
Fix the root connection first, not just the symptom you originally noticed.
Watch the other connected areas after the fix, a real root-cause fix should improve more than one thing.
questions: Which isolated problem are you solving that's actually connected to 3 others?
What's a fix that worked in one area but unexpectedly moved a number somewhere else entirely?
If you mapped every area your current problem touches, would you still call it a single, isolated issue?
content.equation_example: Rate how strongly 3 different areas affect each other, 1-10: Sleep-to-Energy 8, Energy-to-Output 7, Stress-to-Health 6. Sum equals 8 plus 7 plus 6 = 21. Higher totals mean your system has a lot of cross-wired dependency, so fixing one area will ripple into the others; lower totals mean the areas are more isolated, so fixes stay contained.
content.closing_statement: Nothing you fix stays isolated for long.

caption:
Principle Nº 125: Interdependence

Everything affects everything.

Ask yourself: Which isolated problem are you solving that's actually connected to 3 others?
